item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Both the first and second file are owned by Blu, who permits non-commercial and non-derivative usage of their work. As this is not compliant with CC 3.0, both have since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just one,its really cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a Portland native, I can't see this happening in Lake Oswego, really. Too posh of an area for graffiti. Was Port Orford the ground zero for this?

item.title: Re: Credit for the Art
item.content:encoded: Are the pic and the gif for this article licence-free? It would surprise me if Blu doesn't hold any rights for the videos and any/all content coming out them. Also, unanimous artist? You just mentioned their name! In what sense?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The best part about Are We Cool yet is the reveal of the GOI in the article, and this one does it so well.

item.title: Legal stuff.
item.content:encoded:
I feel it's really important to state, in the video that the original artist displayed his artwork through, there was a 15 second disclaimer that specifically stated that while anyone can use this for non profit purposes, it is not allowed to be modified in any way without direct permission from the original artist.

To the author, you gave credit to the original artist for your gif, but you never specified that you got permission to alter the video to make it fit into AWCY. This is actually pretty important, i don't want to even vote on it into this is clarified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn. That gif is something truly special. The concept is great. The philosphy of art is really one of the more fun ones out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 because I thought the AWCY part was well done, -1 because the video felt too conspicuous an example of "wierd thing what I found on the internet."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The video makes it for me :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More relevantly, this video has never been popular enough to be considered to have gone viral. I wrote the SCP first, looked up an animated gif second, and Blu's work is pretty much it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh YEAH? Well, you…you…splutter….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw the video ages ago when it went viral, and I downvote when I see SCPs based on viral videos. Especially if they're edited to add "are we cool yet."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The trick to the people being able to recreate new versions of it is that most people wouldn't want to. However, this is why amnesiacs or death are recommended for the exposed. Likewise it helps explain the euclid rating, since there's a chance the original creator or cohorts are still at large. We can only hope that if they are, they consider this particular art piece to have run its course and now be passe, and that they have moved on to other projects!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Graagh, I had an article very similar to this in the preliminary stages of writing… but this is good. Gladly upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this really memetic, or just infohazardous? It's kind of a fine distinction, but if it is a meme I'm not sure what the idea being transmitted is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The entire concept hinges on the gif, which is very well-done but not the author's work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. Very interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean that personally in order to justify this being Euclid there is far too much information left out. How do the foundation know that 50 of these things won't turn up in every major city in the world tomorrow? There is nothing to say that they have it under control in any way whatsoever. The fact that this is basically a meme spread through sight means horrific possibilities if it ever ends up in a populous area. Far worse than say 682 or 058.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It doesn't sit well with me for some reason that it can be recreated once exposed, simply by "concentrating on the memory and using ordinary acrylic spray paint." Just sounds like this thing would be unstoppable, or at least extremely hard to contain in my mind.

Also, since obviously a moving painting on the wall is going to attract attention, I would expect this thing to never stop moving and growing until direct measures were taken to block it from view. I imagine a world overrun with SCPs and whole huge skyscrapers just filled with these paintings!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to criticize you for just using one of those gifs straight up, but then the AWCY happened at the end and that just tickled the hell out of me.

I do have one question: why is this hazardous when getting into orifices?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That gif looks rather borked in some of the later frames, but I'm impressed enough by this whole thing to upvote, but this strikes more as Keter (bare in mind that if you do change it, we'll have four graffiti Keter articles, and finally one that's related to AWCY).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…but in the case that this is Euclid then I don't think it's written well enough.

…What?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh, I'll obviously leave it up to the author but in the case that this is Euclid then I don't think it's written well enough. This skip is more uncontainable and unpredictable than many keters on the site. The fact that it didn't cause millions of deaths upon creation is beyond me.
Changing to -1 until these issues are addressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By my reading of the class guide, you are partially correct.

Keters are things actively harmful and difficult to control.
Safes will (most likely) not hurt anyone if some reasonable precautions are taken.
Euclids are anything that falls between those two extremes.

That would make this is euclid because if you ever once manage to get it in a box (so to speak) it will stay there; but until that containment is complete it could very easily wipe out life as we know it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Danger/Hate/Ragestate" level is not indicative at all of classification. It just matters how hard it is to contain, and for articles like this, though it can be iffy, it seems to be more Euclid than Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The observer breaths

Should this be "breathes", or is it intentional in the context of the note?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My understanding of classes is that Keter is actively harmful, whereas Euclid can be harmful, but is not primarily so. Keters hunt, chase and capture. Euclids spread and grow. Keter is inimical to life. Euclid just happens to be incompatible with it.

It's basically the difference between holding a hedgehog and a Tasmanian devil. One actively wants to see you in pain. The other just happens to be covered in sharp needles.

Or kudzu. It takes the space of everything else, but it doesn't do that as a supremacy plan or some agenda against everything that's not kudzu.

So the graffiti happens to grow inside people and kill them, but that's just an extrapolation of its usual abnormality, that is growing over everything, not exactly because it was made to kill people from the inside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
overall high ability to control

But this skip can presumably appear literally anywhere at any time and it grows exponentially. If it affects 10 people, it could then affect 100, then 1000 and as soon as you know it an entire cities population has acrylic paint coating their entire insides, very likely causing death. I still think that it would make more sense to take out the fact that it can spread to humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair point, stop motion growth should/would have been mentioned in the description.
I like a challenge…give me a minute…
How about this:

The film was made using a preliminary version of experimental protocols. The cameraman is the only observer, and he pays attention to the graffiti in short bursts then turns off the camera and ignores 1446. The thinking was that this would slow the growth to a manageable rate, or at least mitigate the possibility of total "wildfire" growth.

In-universe, this also should have been mentioned somewhere. But it's possible it is in the more fully detailed research documentation that isn't included in this summary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still doesn't explain why the camera angle is different each shot. It's clearly stop-motion. And even if it were to grow in that way, that would need to have been mentioned in the article, which it isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was some conversation about that with all three classifications being promoted but euclid was settled on because of uncertainty about total containment but overall high ability to control.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In-universe, maybe that is a video clip in real time. 1446 happens to grow in such a way that it resembles stop-motion.
Just a possibility.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Euclid vs. Keter is always a bit tricky. This one honestly could even be safe. I usually follow the locked box rule:

If I can lock it in a box and leave it there indefinitely, it's safe.

If I can lock it in a box, but sometimes have to check up on it to make sure everything is okay, it's euclid.

If I have to have ten highly trained guards constantly staring at the box, ready to react if something happens, it's keter.

This one could just be locked in a box and left. That said, uncontained it could be a real nightmare. I honestly thing that euclid is probably the right designation, since it toes the line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand the trepidation some people have with the gif, but for some reason I like graffiti related skips and this one in particular. I'd have to question the Euclid classification though. If one of these was present in central New York or London and the paint can cover the inside of your body as well as the outside, it seems as if it could possibly kill hundreds of thousands of people.
I'd warily suggest a keter classification, or removing the fact that the paint can spread to humans, especially seeing as they have absolutely no idea behind the pattern of how the graffiti shows up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I upvoted this before I saw the gif. Nice job on this one, I love to see when someone sees an image/gif truly inspiring and writes a cool SCP around it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was a pretty good one, even though the gif doesn't work with my slow-as-all-hell internet connection. Something that I didn't get is the AWCY? reference.

item.title: Those Bastards!
item.content:encoded:
You know, I actually appreciate this point, maybe it's time for AWCY to do a completely benign SCP!

I'm just gonna fix that for you.- Scantron

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, AWCY would so do this and the .gif might be non-standard but it is seriously well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I would have liked it more if it wasn't at all dangerous, because that has some cool implications.

Still, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Blushes shyly….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Three things you should feel proud about this article:

1: First decent memetic skip in a while (correct me if I'm wrong).

2: It's using a (pretty cool) gif and it worked well.

3: Are we cool yet? And yeah, it worked.

For all that, plus some good good writing and a decent hook, you deserve this +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Daaamn. I like the concept, even without the .gif. In fact, I felt that it added and extra layer of interest into the piece. Happily +1'd.

item.title: Re: Answering some questions
item.content:encoded: That's actually a good question. The tone of the story suggests that it should be contemplated as "art" but once you start panicking and thinking, "jesus god this paint's trying to kill me!" it doesn't stop, so as long as you were just thinking about it as a mess, graffiti, anything where you had to consciously consider it, it would trigger the effect. I think this holds since some art, particularly things like graffiti and similar vandalism, can be seen as challenging, intentionally offensive, counter-culture, etc., designed to cause reactions more than just art criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the note, and the gif didn't bug me. It's not super great, but it's kinda neat and the overall article worked for me.

item.title: Re: Answering some questions
item.content:encoded:
I see; I did not think of that. I am not sure what it means when it says the viewer must ''focus on the art''. Do you need to examine it and think about it and try to interpret it? If you think ''this is some paint on the wall and I'm gonna clean it'' are you going to be affected? I think it could be explained a bit more clearly.

At Vendor Xeno: When I said ''stop the observer'', I didn't mean to kill, but just stop them from looking at it. If it's a big group you could use tear gas or just shut off the light if indoors. Agents with goggles can control the people, then you could carefully clean the place, using a robot if you need.
(Coming soon: The Roomba Dry-Ice Blasting module!)

item.title: Re: Answering some questions
item.content:encoded:
@cuddles also

That was kind of my point, but I could have been clearer sorry. If the paint is the same temperature as the background then the goggles will be worse than useless, as the guards now can't see what they are cleaning or containing.

If the paint is still visible when using just the infrared spectrum, but not contagious, that should be stated.

If it is visible in infrared but still contagious then we are back to square one. The goggles, they do nothing (other than make the guard's life harder).

item.title: Answering some questions
item.content:encoded: The goggles, yes, negate the chance of the guards acting as observers. The dry-ice blasting is a very quick, currently used method to strip surfaces of graffiti. It's faster than thinner, cleaner than sand and more ethical than killing the observers which, in the case of a breach, could be any number of people anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still hesitant between a +1 or no vote… OK, +1 because I do like graffitis…

I agree the video is cool, but does not look real. 50 square cm per second seems pretty fast to me and it would not need to be stop motion.

What exactly are 'infrared limited goggle' and why have them?

The way I understand, with infrared you see things according to their temperature and since the graffiti probably has the same temperature as the surface, then you won't distinguish it.

However I'm not sure about the dry-ice blasting. This can clean graffitis, but why is is necessary for the guards to have them equipped at all time? The way I understand, you stop it by stopping the observer, then you can take your time to clean it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much exactly this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't mind that. If it's slow enough, a recorded video would be nigh useless. A stop motion summary like that would be far more useful than "Look! It moved a centimeter! Now let's watch the next hour…" Granted, this seems fast enough that shouldn't be an issue, but I don't see the stop-motion gif as being a problem per se.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in chat, the whole article hinges on the .gif. That being said, this is a fairly good use of AWCY in their actual context (art), so upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've downvoted because while the gif is awesome, it doesn't work in an article. First of all, gifs rarely work in SCPs. Secondly, it's nice and artsy but enormously unprofessional. There's no way a place like the Foundation would use amateur stop-motion to document an anomaly. They would have a high quality camera set up on a tripod, not some dude with an SLR snapping photos every couple of seconds.

Beyond that, the gif really sums up the article, which isn't very interesting beyond it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite like this. I agree we do have other moving/living graffiti (TIL the singular is graffito) but this feels different to them.

  • The quoted box at the seems to have extra line breaks; on my phone you end up with sentences chopped in half.
  • What exactly are 'infrared limited goggle' and why have them? If they allow you to view the skip without coming under its influence that should be explicit.
  • If the dry ice blasting units are for cleaning/controlling growing instances then say that as apart of the containment. The people responsible for containment shouldn't have to infer what they need to do.
  • Unless Protocol 94-Wegner-Gyatso is a sure fire way to limit information leaks, the sentence "This makes SCP-1446 a risk for possible information leaks; security and exposure procedures should reflect this" doesn't really match up with letting level 3 staff or above contact the skip. And if Protocol 94-Wegner-Gyatso does mitigate that problem then this should be clearer in the description.

Edit: The AWCY reference feels a bit tacked on, but not so much I will change my vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Likewise! The gif made the article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That gif was pretty damn cool. Upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The gif is cool as hell, but we've already got several "anomalous graffiti" articles, and I'm not sure it can carry an article on its own.

item.title: Credit for the Art
item.content:encoded:
The original stop motion graffiti was created by anonymous street artist Blu

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blu_(artist)
